Donor 23 was sampled in 2008 for mAbs VRC-PG19 and VRC-PG20; infected with clade A [Zhou2013a, PMID: 23911655]. VRC01 class [VH1-2-derived]-antibodies, in particular VRC-PG19, VRC-PG19b, VRC-PG20, VRC-PG20b, were previously isolated from this donor [Zhou2015, PMID: 26004070]. The Donor was not receiving ART at the time of antibody derivation [Simek2009, PMID: 19439467] [Walker2010, PMID: 20700449] [Personal communication, Dr. Elise Landais].

Participant IDC508 from Germany was found to have CD4bs directed neutralizing antibody activity. At the time of leukapheresis this participant was 37 years old, CDC A2 (asymptomatic), and had been HIV-infected for 8.9 years. Their viral load was 118000 copies/mL and CD4 count 450 cells/mL at the time of blood draw and they were infected by HIV-1 subtype D [Radford2023 PMID: 37327779]. The neutralization activity of IDC508 serum ranked 19th out of 2354 participants, thus classifying them as an elite neutralizer [Schommers2023 PMID: 37957379].

Participant IDC513 from Germany was found to have CD4bs directed neutralizing antibody activity. At the time of leukapheresis this participant was 66 years old and had been HIV-infected for 30.66 years. Their viral load was 42 copies/mL and CD4 count 445 cells/mL at the time of blood draw and they were infected by HIV-1 subtype B [Radford2023 PMID: 37327779]. The neutralization activity of IDC513 serum ranked 90th out of 2354 participants, thus classifying them as an elite neutralizer [Schommers2023 PMID: 37957379].

Participant IDC561, from Cologne, Germany, had neutralization activity in the top 1% of a cohort of 2,274 people living with HIV, thus being classified as an elite neutralizer. IDC561 was described as a clade B-infected long-term non-progressor. Neutralization fingerprinting detected VRC01-like activity that was identified as being different from VRC01-class bnAbs. At the time of leukapheresis this participant was ART naive, 48 years old, CDC A2 (asymptomatic), and had been HIV-infected for 21 years. At 8 weeks prior to leukapheresis (20.6 yrs post-diagnosis) they had viral load 6030 copies/mL and CD4 count 540 cells/mL. Their HLA alleles were A*02 B*13 B*27 [Schommers2020 PMID: 32004464]. Another study reported their viral load 5530 copies/mL and CD4 count 550 cells/mL at 20.2 yrs post-diagnosis [Radford2023 PMID: 37327779]. The neutralization activity of this IDC561 serum ranked 22nd among 2354 participants; the Ab repertoire and dynamics of IDC561 were studied in detail pre- and post-ART initiation [Schommers2023 PMID: 37957379].

Participant IDF033 from Germany was found to have CD4bs directed neutralizing antibody activity. At the time of leukapheresis this participant was 53 years old, CDC stage B3 (symptomatic) and had been HIV-infected for 20.38 years. Their viral load was 19 copies/mL and CD4 count 313 cells/mL at the time of blood draw and they were infected by HIV-1 subtype D [Radford2023 PMID: 37327779]. The neutralization activity of IDF033 serum ranked 20th out of 2354 participants, thus classifying them as an elite neutralizer [Schommers2023 PMID: 37957379].

IK16 was vaccinated with iMac-ΔD385, a non-CD4-tropic variant of SIVmac239, and challenged with SIVsmE660 swarm; plasma from rhesus IK19 neutralized all SIV strains tested with unusually high neutralization titers  [Welles2022].

INME or Patient2 infected at age 25, presented to University of Alabama at Birmingham hospital. 7 and 48 days after onset of symptoms, CD4 count was 739 and 455 cells/ml respectively [Clark1991, PMID: 1900576]. At CDC stage 1 of infection, there were 739 CD4+ cells/mL, and 2.4 x10<sup>6</sup> HIV-1 RNA copies/mL.  [Piatak1993, PMID: 8096089]. Initial HLA serotyping is from Borrow 1994 and infectious virus was first cleared from plasma 81d after onset of acute retroviral syndrome, this patient did not control primary plasma viremia and antigenemia efficiently. [Borrow1994, PMID: 8057491].
Subject JC is from a Boston cohort. [Goulder2000, PMID: 10823876].

Subject JFM is from a Boston cohort. [Goulder2000, PMID: 10823876].  Adult subject chronically infected for 2 years, ART-nil with VL 307 RNA copies/ml plasma and CD4+ T cells at 555 cells/ml with CTL specificity to A2-Gag. [Goulder2000b, PMID:M 11120778].

Transiently Aviremic patient, TA-2, was an ART-naive female child who reported escapes to the Gag TL9 epitope previously. A pattern of transient aviremia followed by viral rebound and CD4+ T-cell decline was observed. TA-2 was followed from birth (Fig. 1B). Similar to TA-1, but at a younger age, TA-2 reached undetectable viremia at the age of 4.2 years, within 3 months rebounding to 3,100 copies/mL. Within 9 months, a new viral set point was maintained at 9,100 copies/mL, and this was followed by a decline in the CD4+ T-cell count, [Vieira2022, PMID: 34757843].

Longitudinally-tracked treatment-naive pediatric subject infected with C clade HIV-1, which was presumably transmitted by mother, K-004-M [Leitman2017, PMID: 28983013]. Transiently Aviremic patient, TA-1, was an ART-naive female child who reported escapes to the Gag TL9 epitope previously. A pattern of transient aviremia followed by viral rebound and CD4+ T-cell decline was observed. She reached aviremia at the age of 8.2 years, rebounding within 3 months to a plasma viral load of 4,100 copies/mL (Fig. 1A). Within 9 months, her viral load had returned to the preexisting set point (median of 17,294 copies/ mL), which was now followed by a progressive decline in her CD4+ T-cell count. TA-2 was followed from birth (Fig. 1B).[Vieira2022, PMID: 34757843].

In an immunization regimen with long intervals, rhesus macaques of Chinese origin, approximately 5 to 6 years old, were immunized 3 times at weeks 0, 8, and 32 by intramuscular injection. Soluble YU2gp140-F Env trimers in combination with AbISCO and CpG ODN2395 adjuvant were used in the inoculation. Two hundred micrograms of Env protein/animal was given for the first inoculation, and 100 ug was given for the following injections. Peripheral blood was collected 2 weeks after each immunization, followed by PBMC preparation. In this study, frozen PBMCs from rhesus macaque K17 at 2 weeks after the third immunization were used to derive a large panel of mAbs, including S30, S80, S96, S98, S100, and S152 [Wang2017 PMID: 28835491].

CTL specific for Gag28-36 epitope (KYKLKHIVW) escape 3R(K30R) mutant (KYrLKHIVW) from this HIV-1-infected, HLA-A*2402+ Japanese patient (1 of 12) were monitored for response from early to chronic phase of infection. KI-119, a chronically infected patient, had immune responses to 3 peptides, FV9, FP10, FV11. Unlike other patients studied, KI-119 did not have CD8+ T cells expressing TCRs with much stronger affinity for the FV9 than for the FP10 peptide though CTL clones from KI-119 did bind both FP10 and FV9. Immunogenicity to FP10 and FV11 was very low. [Hashimoto2012, PMID: 22740036].

Subject KI-1506, chronically infected with HIV-1 & ART-naive, was sampled with a pVL of 22,000 RNA copies/mL and CD4 count of 515 cells/mm<sup>3</sup>. [Paes2019, PMID: 31748275]

CTL specific for Gag28-36 epitope (KYKLKHIVW) escape 3R(K30R) mutant (KYrLKHIVW) from this HIV-1-infected, HLA-A*2402+ Japanese patient (1 of 12) were monitored for response from early to chronic phase of infection. Seroconversion confirmed within 6 months. 2 sampling dates between 5/2/2001 and 8/28/2003 found autologous virus 3R mutants. Gag28 specific-CTL from patient KI-151 cultured with 3R peptide produced IFN-γ in response to stimulation by 3R peptide but not WT.
Subject KI-1527, chronically infected with HIV-1 & ART-naive, was sampled with a pVL of 41,000 RNA copies/mL and CD4 count of 99 cells/mm<sup>3</sup>. [Paes2019, PMID: 31748275]

CTL specific for Gag28-36 epitope (KYKLKHIVW) escape 3R(K30R) mutant (KYrLKHIVW) from this HIV-1-infected, HLA-A*2402+ Japanese patient (1 of 12) were monitored for response from early to chronic phase of infection. Seroconversion confirmed within 6 months. 1 sampling date on 4/12/2002 found autologous virus 3R mutants.
Subject KI-1545, chronically infected with HIV-1 & ART-naive, was sampled with a pVL of 5,700 RNA copies/mL and CD4 count of 508 cells/mm<sup>3</sup>. [Paes2019, PMID: 31748275]

Patient KI-158 had 500 copies/ml viral load and 958 CD4 cells/µl on 07/17/03, and carrying the RYPiTFGW(CF)  mutations. On 10/01/04 patient KI-158 had 19,000 copies/ml viral load (VL) and 464 CD4 cells/µl, and carried the RfPiTFGW(CF) mutation. By 02/01/05 KI-158 has a VL of 1,200 copies/ml and CD4 cells/µl had sunk to 398.  CTL specific for Gag28-36 epitope (KYKLKHIVW) and/or its escape 3R(K30R) mutant (KYrLKHIVW) from this HIV-1-infected, HLA-A*2402+ Japanese patient (1 of 12) were monitored for response from early to chronic phase of infection. Seroconversion confirmed within 6 months. 7 sampling dates between 6/14/2002 and 4/4/2005 found autologous virus as WT and later, 3R mutants. Gag28 specific-CTL from patient KI-158 cultured with WT or 3R peptide produced IFN-γ in response to stimulation by either WT or 3R peptide.

KI-160, a chronically infected patient, showed positive EliSpot responses to 3 peptides, FV9, FP10, FV11. Though FV9 was the optimal epitope, it was not most frequently detected by CTL from KI-160. [Hashimoto2012, PMID: 22740036].

Patient KI-161 had 500 copies/ml viral load and 958 CD4 cells/µl on 07/17/03, and carrying the RYPiTFGW(CF) mutations. On 10/01/04 patient KI-161 had 19,000 copies/ml viral load and 464 CD4 cells/µl, and carried the RfPiTFGW(CF) mutation.  CTL specific for Gag28-36 epitope (KYKLKHIVW) and/or its escape 3R(K30R) mutant (KYrLKHIVW) from this HIV-1-infected, HLA-A*2402+ Japanese patient (1 of 12) were monitored for response from early to chronic phase of infection. Seroconversion confirmed within 6 months. 4 sampling dates between 2/15/2002 and 6/16/2005 found autologous virus as WT and later, 3R mutants. Gag28 specific-CTL from patient KI-161 cultured with WT or 3R peptide produced IFN-γ in response to stimulation by either WT or 3R peptide.

CTL specific for Gag28-36 epitope (KYKLKHIVW) escape 3R(K30R) mutant (KYrLKHIVW) from this HIV-1-infected, HLA-A*2402+ Japanese patient (1 of 12) were monitored for response from early to chronic phase of infection. Seroconversion confirmed within 6 months. 2 sampling dates between 8/30/2002 and 9/27/2004 found autologous virus 3R mutants. Gag28 specific-CTL from patient KI-163 cultured at the later time point with 3R peptide produced IFN-γ in response to stimulation by 3R peptide but not WT.

Patient KI-167 was ART-naive and had 26,000 copies/ml viral load, 455 CD4 cells/µl, and 909 CD8 cells/µl. KI-167 did not carry Pol283-8-specific cells (as determined by ICS), and they did not recognize the epitope when used as ex-vivo PBMC in EliSpot. [Yagita2013 PMID: 23236061].

KI-172, a chronically infected patient, had immune responses to 3 peptides, FV9, FP10, FV11. CTL clones from KI-172 had different specificities: clone 21-10 bound FT8 (FPISPIET), FV9, FP10; clone 13-10 bound FV9 and FP10; clone 37-10 bound FP10 and FV11 and not FV9, which is usually optimal. [Hashimoto2012, PMID: 22740036].
The evolution of Gag RI8 escape mutant RMYSPsSI from RMYSPTSI was observed in treatment-naive chronically infected subject KI-194.  [Zhang2020, PMID: 32699092]

Treatment-naive subject, KI-376, chronically infected with variant Gag RI8 RMYSPaSI virus, had a RMYSPTSI-specific CTL response (IFNγ EliSpot, >600 spots/10<sup>6</sup> CTLs) but failed to respond to variants RMYSPsSI and RMYSPaSI [Zhang2020, PMID: 32699092]. Subtype B-infected KI-376 was recruited between 2007 and 2019. HLA alleles published as A1:2402, A2:-, B1:0702, B2:5201, C1:0702, and C2:1202 [Zhang2022b, PMID: 36326273].
KI-382 is an A*26:02+ subtype B-infected HIV Japanese patient in the chronic stage of infection. [Kawashima2008, PMID: 25305317].

Patient KI-388 carries HLAs A*0206 and B*4002 and is a chronically infected responder with clade A/E HIV-1. Epitopes Gag LL1-1M-4L (MKHLVWASREL), Pol GI9 (GQVDCSPGI), Gag RV8-8L (RELERFAL), Gag AP8-6V (AEWDRVHP), Gag AV9-6V (AEWDRVHPV) and Gag EG11-5V (EWDRVHPVHAG) were identified in the patient.
Patient KI-472 was a chronic progressor who carried PolGI8, GERIVDII, and whose GI8-specific CTL cross-recognized all variant form GERIiDII (5I), but not variant form GERIVDmI (7M).

Murakoshi2019 reports that CTL specific for epitope TPQDLNTML(TL9) from WT HIV-1-infected KI-474 were generated into T cell clone lines. Treatment-naive subject KI-474 was chronically infected with HIV-1 subtype B and was recruited between 2007 and 2019. Full HLA alleles reported [Zhang2022b, PMID: 36326273].

Murakoshi2019 reports that CTL specific for epitope NPDCKTILKAL (NL11) from WT HIV-1-infected KI-475 were generated into T cell clone lines. Treatment-naive subject KI-475 was chronically infected with HIV-1 subtype B and was recruited between 2007 and 2019. Full HLA alleles reported [Zhang2022b, PMID: 36326273].
KI-478 is an A*26:02+ subtype B-infected HIV Japanese patient in the chronic stage of infection. [Kawashima2008, PMID: 25305317].
Murakoshi2019 reports that KI-498 has no CTL response to SV9 (SQIYAGIKV) but does respond to SV9-5P/5P9I(SQIYpGIKV/SQIYpGIKi).  KI-498 is not infected with WT SV9 but with SV9-5P, SQIYpGIKV.

Patient KI-504 was a subtype B-chronically infected HLA-Cw*1202 subject who was 1 of 3/80 who had a CTL response to Cw*1202-restricted epitope Pol KY9, KQNPDIVIY(Chikata2017). KI-504 responds to HIV-1 RT173-181 Cw*1202-restricted epitope, KY9, KQNPDIVIY, but weakly to KY9 mutant KQNPDIVIc(Kuse2015).  Treatment-naive subject KI-504 infected with Gag RI8 RMYSPvSI-virus had positive CTL IFNγ EliSpot responses to RMYSPvSI and RMYSPTSI (648 and 408 spots/10<sub>6</sub> CTLs, respectively).  [Zhang2020, PMID: 32699092]

Treatment-naive subject, KI-543, chronically infected with variant Gag RI8 RMYSPaSI virus, had a RMYSPTSI-specific CTL response (IFNγ EliSpot, about 250 spots/10<sup>6</sup> CTLs) but failed to respond to variants RMYSPsSI and RMYSPaSI. [Zhang2020, PMID: 32699092]
Murakoshi2019 reports that KI-551 has no CTL response to SV9 (SQIYAGIKV) but does respond to SV9-5P/5P9I(SQIYpGIKV/SQIYpGIKi).  KI-551 is not infected with WT SV9 but with SV9-5P, SQIYpGIKV.
Treatment-naive subject KI-553 was chronically infected with HIV-1 subtype B and was recruited between 2007 and 2019. Full HLA alleles reported [Zhang2022b, PMID: 36326273].
NefT80N mutation, which had a weak but significant association with HLA-B*67:01, was observed in HIV-1 Nef sequences from subtype B-infected subject KI-588 [Zhang2022b, PMID: 36326273].
Murakoshi2019 reports that KI-589 has CTL response to SV9 (SQIYAGIKV) and SV9-5S (SQIYsGIKV) but not to SV9-5P/5P9I.  KI-589 is infected with WT SV9 but not mutant SV9-5P.

Murakoshi2019 reports that CTL specific for epitope ATLEEMMTA(AA9) from WT HIV-1-infected KI-611 were generated into T cell clone lines. Treatment-naive KI-611 was infected with HIV-1 subtype B and was recruited between 2007 and 2019. HLA alleles published as A1:0206, A2:3101, B1:0702, B2:1501, C1:0401, and C2:0702 [Zhang2022b, PMID: 36326273]

Treatment-naive subject KI-619 was infected with HIV-1 subtype B and was recruited between 2007 and 2019. HLA alleles published as A1:3101, A2:3303, B1:3901, B2:4403, C1:0702, and C2:1403 [Zhang2022b, PMID: 36326273].
Murakoshi2019 reports that bulk T cells specific for epitope IEAEVIPAET(IT10) were established from KI-620.

Treatment-naive subject, KI-621, chronically infected with Gag RI8 RMYSPvSI-virus had a strong RMYSPTSI-specific CTL response (IFNγ EliSpot, 5721 spots/10<sup>6</sup> CTLs) but failed to respond to RMYSPvSI.  [Zhang2020, PMID: 32699092]

KI-627, a chronically infected patient, showed positive EliSpot responses to 3 peptides, FV9, FP10, FV11.  KI-627 had CD8+ T cells expressing TCRs with much stronger affinity for the FV9 than for the FP10 peptide. [Hashimoto2012, PMID: 22740036].

Murakoshi2019 reports that CTL specific for epitope SQYALGII(SI8) from WT HIV-1-infected KI-628 were generated into T cell clone lines.  Treatment-naive subject, KI-628, chronically infected with Gag RI8 RMYSPvSI-virus had a strong RMYSPvSI-specific CTL IFNγ EliSpot response (1771 spots/10<sup>6</sup> CTLs).  A KI-628-derived RMYSPvSI-specific CTL clone had strong suppressive ability against RMYSPvSI-infected T cells (100%) and moderate suppressive activity against RMYSPYSI-infected T cells (about 40%). [Zhang2020, PMID: 32699092]

Patient KI-632 carries HLAs A*2402 and A*3303 and is a chronically infected responder with clade A/E HIV-1. Epitopes Nef RF10 (RYPLCFGWCF-5C), Pol ER10 (ELKKIIGQVR) and Pol FR9 (FYVDGAASR-8S) were identified in the patient.
Subtype B-infected KI-634 responds to HIV-1 RT180-189 A*0201-restricted epitope, IV10, IYQYMDDLYV, as well as to IV10 mutants IiQYMDDLYV, IvQYMDDLYV, and IcQYMDDLYV (Kuse2015).

Treatment-naive subject, KI-638, chronically infected with variant Gag RI8 RMYSPsSI virus, had a RMYSPTSI-specific CTL response (IFNγ EliSpot, >600 spots/10<sup>6</sup> CTLs) but did not respond to variants RMYSPsSI and RMYSPaSI. [Zhang2020, PMID: 32699092]

ART-naive, WT HIV-1-infected HLA-B*3501+ Japanese subject KI-642 was the donor for CTLs used to produce two YF9 (YPLTFGWCF)-specific clones, H3 and B7.  H3 showed significantly stronger recognition of YF9 peptide over its 1F mutant, while B7 recognized both and mounted a IFN-γ response equally well (Murakoshi2018a). Sampling date was 22April, 2009, at which point pVL = 2400 copies/mL with CD4+ T cell count at 518 cells/µL and CD8+ T cell count at 917 cells/µL, CD4/CD8 ratio = 0.56.

Patient KI-643 was a chronic progressor who carried the variant form of PolSV9, SV9-5S, SQIYsGIKV, and whose SV9-specific CTL did not cross-recognize two variant forms of SV9 viz. SQIYpGIKV (5P) and SQIYpGIKi (5P9I).

Patient KI-647 elicits response to and carries HIV-1 Pol epitope WQRPLVTI. Full HLA alleles published [Murakoshi2017, PMID: 27903797]. Subtype B-infected KI-647 was infected with subtype B [Zhang2022b, PMID: 36326273].

Patient KI-648 carries HLA-A*0206 and is a chronically infected responder with clade A/E HIV-1. Epitopes Nef GL9 (GAFDLSFFL-3F-7F), Gag AA9 (ATLEEMMTA) and Pol YI9 (YTAFTIPSI) were identified in the patient.

Treatment-naive subject KI-653 was infected with HIV-1 subtype B and was recruited between 2007 and 2019. HLA alleles published as A1:2402, A2:3303, B1:0702, B2:4403, C1:0702, and C2:1403 [Zhang2022b, PMID: 36326273].
Subtype A/E-infected patient KI-667 responded to HLA-B*3501-restricted subtype A/E RT175-183 epitope NY9-3E, NPEIVIYQY and cross recognized mutants NPEIVIiQY, NPEIVIvQY, and NPEIVIcQY (Kuse2015).
KI-669, a chronically infected patient, had immune responses to only one of all tested peptides, FV9. [Hashimoto2012, PMID: 22740036].
KI-676, a chronically infected patient, had immune responses to 3 peptides, FV9, FP10, FV11 (very low immunogenicity) [Hashimoto2012, PMID: 22740036].

Treatment-naive subject KI-685 was infected with HIV-1 subtype B and was recruited between 2007 and 2019. HLA alleles published as A1:0201, A2:-, B1:0702, B2:1511, C1:0303, and C2:0702 [Zhang2022b, PMID: 36326273].
Murakoshi2019 reports that CTL specific for epitope WMTETLLV(WV8) from WT HIV-1-infected KI-774 were generated into T cell clone lines.
Treatment-naive subject KI-690 was chronically infected with HIV-1 subtype B and was recruited between 2007 and 2019. Full HLA alleles reported [Zhang2022b, PMID: 36326273].
Subtype B-infected KI-691 responds to HIV-1 RT180-189 A*0201-restricted epitope, IV10, IYQYMDDLYV, but not to IV10 mutants IiQYMDDLYV, IvQYMDDLYV, and IcQYMDDLYV (Kuse2015).

Patient KI-692 was a chronic progressor who carried the wild type form of Gag RI8, RMYSPTSI, and whose RI8-specific CTL did not cross-recognize variant forms of RI8 viz. MYSPvSI (6V), RMYSPsSI (6S), RMYSPaSI (6A), RMYSPiSI.

Patient KI-698 was a chronically infected HLA-Cw*1202 subject who was 1 of 3/80 who had a CTL response to Cw*1202-restricted epitope Pol KY9 (KQNPDIVIY). The patient elicits response to and carries HIV-1 Pol epitope WQRPLVTI. [Murakoshi2017, PMID: 27903797]. Treatment-naive subject, KI-698, chronically infected with Gag RI8 RMYSPvSI-virus had a RMYSPvSI-specific CTL IFNγ EliSpot positive response (677 spots/10<sup>6</sup> CTLs).  A KI-698-derived RMYSPvSI-specific CTL clone had strong suppressive ability against RMYSPvSI-infected T cells (100%) and mild suppressive activity against RMYSPTSI-infected T cells (about 30%). [Zhang2020, PMID: 32699092]
Treatment-naive subject KI-699 was chronically infected with HIV-1 subtype B and was recruited between 2007 and 2019. Full HLA alleles reported [Zhang2022b, PMID: 36326273].
Treatment-naive subject, KI-705, chronically infected with Gag RI8 RMYSPvSI-virus had a strong CTL IFNγ EliSpot response to RMYSPvSI (2614 spots/10<sup>6</sup> CTLs). [Zhang2020, PMID: 32699092]
Murakoshi2019 reports that CTL specific for epitopes GERIVDII and its variant GERIiDII(GI8) from GI8-5I(GERIiDII)-HIV-1-infected KI-706 were generated into T cell clone lines.

Patient KI-708 had 50,000 copies/ml viral load and 431 CD4 cells/µl on 11/16/09, and carried the RfPLTFGW(CF) mutation [Sun2016, PMID: 27239036]. Treatment-naive KI-708 was infected with HIV-1 subtype B and was recruited between 2007 and 2019 [Zhang2022b, PMID: 36326273].

Treatment-naive subject KI-709 was infected with HIV-1 subtype B and was recruited between 2007 and 2019. HLA alleles published as A1:1101, A2:2402, B1:0702, B2:4001, C1:0702, and C2:- [Zhang2022b, PMID: 36326273].
Patient KI-724 carries HLAs B*5201 and Cw*0102 and is a chronically infected responder with clade A/E HIV-1. Epitopes Gag YI9-4V (YSPVSILDI) and Gag RI8-6V (RMYSPVSI) were identified in the patient.
Patient KI-740 elicits response to and carries HIV-1 Pol epitope WQRPLVTI. [Murakoshi2017, PMID: 27903797].
Patient KI-756 was a chronic progressor who carried PolLA9, LEGKIILVA, and whose LA9-specific CTL cross-recognized variant form LEGKvILVA (5V).

Patient KI-763 was a chronic progressor who carried the variant form of PolSV9, SV9-5S, SQIYsGIKV, and whose SV9-specific CTL cross-recognized all 3 variant forms of SV9 tested viz. SQIYsGIKV (5S), SQIYpGIKV (5P) and SQIYpGIKi (5P9I). Patient KI-763 also carried Pol LA9 LEGKIILVA and LA9-specific CTL cross-recognized variant LEGKvILVA (5V). This patient carried Pol GI8-5I variant GERIiDII and their CTL cross-recognized GI8 GERIVDII and GI8-5I.

Chikata2017 reports that KI-774 was chronically infected with subtype B HIV-1.  CTL clones from KI-774 recognized epitopes MY9 and QA11. Murakoshi2019 reports that CTL specific for epitopes MQMLKETI(MI8), WMTETLLV(WV8) and SQYALGII(SI8) from WT HIV-1-infected KI-774 were generated into T cell clone lines.
Subtype B-infected KI-775 responds to HIV-1 RT180-189 A*0201-restricted epitope, IV10, IYQYMDDLYV, but not to IV10 mutants IiQYMDDLYV, IvQYMDDLYV, and IcQYMDDLYV (Kuse2015).
Murakoshi2019 reports that CTL specific for epitope SQIYAGIKV(SV9) from WT HIV-1-infected KI-776 were generated into T cell clone lines.

Treatment-naive subject KI-782 was infected with HIV-1 subtype B and was recruited between 2007 and 2019. HLA alleles published as A1:0207, A2:2402, B1:0702, B2:4601, C1:0102, and C2:0702 [Zhang2022b, PMID: 36326273].

Treatment-naive subject, KI-784, chronically infected with variant Gag RI8 RMYSPsSI virus, had a RMYSPTSI-specific CTL response (IFNγ EliSpot, about 210 spots/10<sup>6</sup> CTLs) but failed to respond to RMYSPsSI and RMYSPaSI. [Zhang2020, PMID: 32699092]
Treatment-naive subject KI-794 was chronically infected with HIV-1 subtype B and was recruited between 2007 and 2019. Full HLA alleles reported [Zhang2022b, PMID: 36326273].
Treatment-naive subject, KI-797, chronically infected with Gag RI8 RMYSPvSI-virus had a positive CTL IFNγ EliSpot response to RMYSPvSI (360 spots/10<sup>6</sup> CTLs). [Zhang2020, PMID: 32699092]
Patient KI-801 was a chronically infected HLA-Cw*1202 subject who was 1 of 3/80 who had a CTL response to Cw*1202-restricted epitope Pol KY9 (KQNPDIVIY).
Subtype A/E-infected patient KI-805 responded to HLA-B*3501-restricted subtype A/E RT175-183 epitope NY9-3E, NPEIVIYQY and cross recognized mutants NPEIVIiQY, NPEIVIvQY, and NPEIVIcQY (Kuse2015).
Murakoshi2019 reports that CTL specific for epitopes LEGKIILVA(LA9) and IEAEVIPAET(IT10) from WT HIV-1-infected KI-808 were generated into T cell clone lines.

Treatment-naive subject, KI-809, chronically infected with Gag RI8 RMYSPTSI had a strong RMYSPTSI-specific CTL response but failed to respond to variants RMYSPsSI, RMYSPaSI and RMYSPvSI.  Two KI-809-derived RMYSPTSI-specific CTL clones failed to recognize RMYSPsSI and RMYSPaSI. [Zhang2020, PMID: 32699092]

Patient KI-829 was a chronic progressor who carried the wild type form of Gag RI8, RMYSPTSI, and whose RI8-specific CTL did not cross-recognize variant forms of RI8 viz. MYSPvSI (6V), RMYSPsSI (6S), RMYSPaSI (6A), RMYSPiSI.
Subtype B-infected patient KI-833 responded to HLA-B*3501-restricted RT175-183 epitope NY9, NPDIVIYQY but not to any mutants NPDIVIiQY, NPDIVIvQY, or NPDIVIcQY (Kuse2015).

Patient KI-837 carries HLA-B*4002 and is a chronically infected responder with clade A/E HIV-1. Epitope GI9-5I,GERIIDII, was identified in the patient (Watanabe2013). KI-837 CTL respond to HLA-A*0201-restricted, RT180-189 epitope IV10, IYQYMDDLYV but not to mutants tested.
Subtype B-infected KI-840 responds to HIV-1 RT180-189 A*0201-restricted epitope, IV10, IYQYMDDLYV, but not to IV10 mutants IiQYMDDLYV, IvQYMDDLYV, and IcQYMDDLYV (Kuse2015).

Treatment-naive subject KI-846 was infected with HIV-1 subtype B and was recruited between 2007 and 2019. HLA alleles published as A1:0201, A2:0206, B1:4001, B2:5101, C1:0702, and C2:1502 [Zhang2022b, PMID: 36326273].
Chikata2017 reports that KI-848 was treatment-naive and chronically infected with subtype B HIV-1.
Treatment-naive subject, KI-852, chronically infected with Gag RI8 RMYSPvSI-virus had a positive CTL IFNγ EliSpot response to RMYSPvSI (234 spots/10<sup>6</sup> CTLs). [Zhang2020, PMID: 32699092]

Treatment-naive subject KI-855, chronically infected with Gag RI8 RMYSPvSI-virus had an incomplete viral reversion (RMYSPvSI & RMYSPTSI observed) by December 2010 when CTL clone lines were established. By September 2013, only RMYSPTSI was observed (complete reversion). KI-855 derived RMYSPvSI-specific CTL clones only recognized RMYSPvSI but some RMYSPTSI-specific CTL clones were cross-reactive to RMYSPvSI [Zhang2020, PMID: 32699092]. KI-699 was infected with HIV-1 subtype B and was recruited between 2007 and 2019. Full HLA alleles reported [Zhang2022b, PMID: 36326273].
Subject KI-871 was infected with HIV-1 subtype B [Zhang2022b, PMID: 36326273].

Treatment-naive subject KI-872 was infected with HIV-1 subtype B and was recruited between 2007 and 2019. HLA alleles published as A1:2601, A2:-, B1:0702, B2:5401, C1:0102, and C2:0702 [Zhang2022b, PMID: 36326273].
Murakoshi2019 reports that KI-878 has CTL response to IT10 (IEAEVIPAET), as well as variants IT10-1M(mEAEVIPAET) and IT10-1L(lEAEVIPAET), being infected with the WT and/or mutants.

Murakoshi2019 reports that KI-887 has CTL response to SV9 (SQIYAGIKV) and SV9-5S (SQIYsGIKV) but not to SV9-5P/5P9I.  KI-887 is infected with WT SV9 but not mutant SV9-5P. Autologous virus sequence from KI-887 is accession number LC427217.
Murakoshi2019 reports that KI-889 has no CTL response to SV9 (SQIYAGIKV) but does respond to SV9-5P/5P9I(SQIYpGIKV/SQIYpGIKi).  KI-889 is not infected with WT SV9 but with SV9-5P, SQIYpGIKV.
Patient KI-894 elicits response to and carries HIV-1 Pol epitope WQRPLVTI. [Murakoshi2017, PMID: 27903797].

Patient KI-902 was a chronic progressor who carried the wild type form of Gag RI8, RMYSPTSI, and whose RI8-specific CTL did not cross-recognize variant forms of RI8 viz. MYSPvSI (6V), RMYSPsSI (6S), RMYSPaSI (6A), RMYSPiSI.

Treatment-naive subject, KI-903, chronically infected with Gag RI8 RMYSPvSI-virus had a strong RMYSPvSI-specific CTL response (IFNγ EliSpot, 3065 spots/10<sup>6</sup> CTLs).  KI-903-derived RMYSPvSI-specific CTL clone had strong suppressive ability against RMYSPvSI-infected T cells (100%) and mild suppressive activity against RMYSPTSI-infected T cells (about 25%). [Zhang2020, PMID: 32699092]

Treatment-naive subject, KI-904, chronically infected with Gag RI8 RMYSPvSI-virus had positive CTL IFNγ EliSpot responses to RMYSPvSI and RMYSPTSI (555 and 7925 spots/10<sup>6</sup> CTLs, respectively). [Zhang2020, PMID: 32699092]

Patient KI-906 elicits response to and carries HIV-1 Pol epitope WQRPLVTI. [Murakoshi2017, PMID: 27903797]. In August 2013, the evolution of Gag RI8 escape mutant RMYSPvSI from RMYSPTSI was observed in treatment-naive chronically infected subject KI-906. RMYSPTSI-specific CTL clones derived from KI-906 both before and after the observation of RMYSPvSI failed to recognize RMYSPvSI or suppress RMYSPvSI-virus. [Zhang2020, PMID: 32699092]
Patient KI-909 was a chronic progressor who carried PolGI8, GERIVDII, and whose GI8-specific CTL cross-recognized all variant form GERIiDII (5I), but not variant form GERIVDmI (7M).

Patient KI-912 was a chronic progressor who carried the variant form of PolSV9, SV9-5P8R, SQIYpGIrV, and whose CTL cross-recognized all 3 variant forms of SV9 tested viz. SQIYsGIKV (5S), SQIYpGIKV (5P) and SQIYpGIKi (5P9I). Patient KI-912 also carried Pol LEGKIILVA (LA9) as well as its variant LEGKvILVA (LA9-5V) and specific CTL cross-recognized variant LA9-5V with wild type LEGKIILVA. Also see Murakoshi2019.

Treatment-naive subject, KI-917, chronically infected with Gag RI8 RMYSPvSI-virus had positive CTL IFNγ EliSpot responses to RMYSPvSI (305 spots/10<sup>6</sup> CTLs) and RMYSPTSI (408 spots/10<sup>6</sup> CTLs).  A KI-917-derived RMYSPvSI-specific CTL clone had strong suppressive ability against both RMYSPvSI-infected T cells (100%) and RMYSPTSI-infected T cells (about 90%). [Zhang2020, PMID: 32699092]
Patient KI-926 was a chronic progressor who carried PolSV9, SQIYAGIKV, and whose SV9-specific CTL cross-recognized all 3 variant forms tested SQIYpGIKi (5P9I), SQIYpGIKV (5P) and SQIYsGIKV (5S).

Patient KI-928 was a chronic progressor who carried PolLA9, LEGKIILVA, and whose LA9-specific CTL cross-recognized variant form LEGKvILVA (5V). Murakoshi2019 reports that KI-928 has CTL response to IT10 (IEAEVIPAET), as well as variants IT10-1M(mEAEVIPAET) and IT10-1L(lEAEVIPAET), being infected with the WT and/or mutants.
Subtype B-infected patient KI-929 responded to HLA-B*3501-restricted RT175-183 epitope NY9, NPDIVIYQY but not to any mutants NPDIVIiQY, NPDIVIvQY, or NPDIVIcQY (Kuse2015).
NefT80N mutation, which had a weak but significant association with HLA-B*67:01, was observed in HIV-1 Nef sequences from subtype B-infected subject KI-960 [Zhang2022b, PMID: 36326273].
Patient KI-964 carries HLA-A*0206 and is a chronically infected responder with clade A/E HIV-1. Epitopes Pol SV9 (SQIYAGIKV) and Pol GL9 (GQETAYFLL) were identified in the patient.
Murakoshi2019 reports that bulk T cells specific for epitope SQIYAGIKV(SV9) was established from KI-966.
The evolution of Gag RI8 escape mutant RMYSPaSI from RMYSPTSI was observed in treatment-naive chronically infected subject KI-973.  [Zhang2020, PMID: 32699092]
Subtype B-infected patient KI-980 responded to HLA-B*3501-restricted RT175-183 epitope NY9, NPDIVIYQY but not to any mutants NPDIVIiQY, NPDIVIvQY, or NPDIVIcQY (Kuse2015).

A male individual diagnosed in 1989 and presenting in 2023 (at age 58) with multi-drug resistant HIV and Kaposi sarcoma was treated with a new antiretroviral regimen consisting of anti-CD4 domain 1 antibody UB-421 and capsid inhibitor lenacapavir. The participant’s baseline viral isolates (before initiation of UB-421 and lenacapavir) were highly resistant to 6 HIV-specific broadly neutralizing antibodies (bNAbs) being considered for clinical development (3BNC117, VRC01, VRC07, N6, PGDM1400), while being moderately resistant to 10-1074 and iMab, and susceptible to 10E8 and UB-421.  [Rai2025, PMID: 39753965].

141 months after enrollment in 1985 as an HIV-1 infected subject in the Amsterdam cohort, LTNP L1's CD4 count was 300 cells/μl. HLA alleles reported as A*01, A*03, B*5701, and B*5702. L1 was heterozygous for the CCR5 32bp deletion [Navis2007, PMID: 17709528]. L1 was therapy naive during study period and had a VL of <1,000 copies/ml at 141 mo after enrollment [Navis2008a, PMID: 18279072].

21 months after enrollment in 1985 as an HIV-1 infected subject in the Amsterdam cohort, LTNP L2's CD4 count was 530 cells/μl. HLA alleles reported as A*01, A*24, B*5701, and B*1501. L2 did not carry a copy of the CCR5 32bp deletion [Navis2007, PMID: 17709528]. L2 was therapy naive during study period and had a VL of <1,000 copies/ml at 21 mo after enrollment [Navis2008a, PMID: 18279072].

LTNP L3's CD4 count was 330 cells/μl 115 months after HIV-1 seroconversion, which occurred in 1986. HLA alleles reported as A*02, A*03, B7, B*58:01. L3 did not carry a copy of the CCR5 32bp deletion [Navis2007, PMID: 17709528]. L3 was therapy naive during study period and had a VL of <1,000 copies/ml at 115 mo after seroconversion [Navis2008a, PMID: 18279072].

132 months after enrollment in 1989 as an HIV-1 infected subject in the Amsterdam cohort, LTNP L4's CD4 count was 230 cells/μl. L4 did not carry a copy of the CCR5 32bp deletion [Navis2007, PMID: 17709528]. L4 was therapy naive during study period and had a VL of 1,600 copies/ml at 132 mo after enrollment [Navis2008a, PMID: 18279072].

89 and 177 months after enrollment in 1984 as an HIV-1 infected subject in Amsterdam cohort, LTNP L5's CD4 counts were 660 and 320 cells/μl, respectively. HLA alleles reported as A*26, A*6801, B*57, B*0702. L5 did not carry a copy of the CCR5 32bp deletion [Navis2007, PMID: 17709528]. L1 was therapy naive during study period and had VLs of <1,000 and 5,800 copies/ml, respectively, at 89 and 177 mo after enrollment [Navis2008a, PMID: 18279072]. VL and T cell counts over time for slow progressor B57-SP23 are shown in Supplemental Figure 1. HLA-C allele reported as Cw*07 [Koning2022, PMID: 34880106].

17 and 114 months after enrollment in 1988 as an HIV-1 infected subject in Amsterdam cohort, LTNP L6's CD4 counts were 750 and 380 cells/μl, respectively. HLA alleles reported as A3, A11, B*5801, and B7. L6 was heterozygous for the CCR5 32bp deletion [Navis2007, PMID: 17709528]. L6 was therapy naive during study period and had VLs of <1,000 and 5,000 copies/ml at 17 and 114 mo after enrollment [Navis2008a, PMID: 18279072]. VL and T cell counts over time for slow progressor B57-SP19 are shown in Supplemental Figure 1. HLA-C alleles published as Cw*01 and Cw*07 [Koning2022, PMID: 34880106].

26, 78, 102, and 136 months after enrollment in 1984 as an HIV-1 infected subject in the Amsterdam cohort, LTNP L7's CD4 counts were 940, 770, 460 and 270 cells/μl, respectively. X4-tropic variants were detected at 102 months post enrollment. HLA alleles reported as A*01, A*02, B*5701, and B*0801. L7 was heterozygous for the CCR5 32bp deletion [Navis2007, PMID: 17709528]. L7 was therapy naive during study period and had VLs of 26,000, <1000, 3,200, and 54,000 copies/ml, respectively, at 26, 78, 102, and 136 mo after enrollment [Navis2008a, PMID: 18279072]. VL and T cell counts over time for slow progressor B57-SP21 are shown in Supplemental Figure 1. HLA-C alleles published as Cw*0602 and Cw*07 [Koning2022, PMID: 34880106].

70, 91 and 137 months after becoming HIV-1 seropositive in 1990, LTNP L8's CD4 counts were 840, 630 and 290 cells/μl, respectively. HLA alleles reported as A*02, A*02, B*5701, and B*3701. L8 did not carry the CCR5 32bp deletion [Navis2007, PMID: 17709528]. L8 was therapy naive during study period and had VLs of <1,000, 9,800, and 16,470 copies/ml at 70, 91 and 137 mo after enrollment [Navis2008a, PMID: 18279072]. VL and T cell counts over time are shown for slow progressor B57-SP20 in Supplemental Figure 1. HLA-C allele published as Cw*0602 [Koning2022, PMID: 34880106].
LongT9 is an individual with HIV infection who has maintained an average CD4 count of 1246 cells/μL and an average of 6568 HIV-1 RNA copies/mL for almost 10 yr. [Guan2013, PMID: 23237851].

42, 59 and 77 months after becoming HIV-1 seropositive in 1985, LTNP L9's CD4 counts were 610, 740, and 770 cells/μl, respectively. HLA alleles reported as A*01, A*6802, B*5701, and B*1402. L9 did not carry the CCR5 32bp deletion [Navis2007, PMID: 17709528]. L9 was therapy naive during study period and had a VL of <1,000 copies/ml at each time point of 41, 59 and 77 mo after enrollment [Navis2008a, PMID: 18279072]. VL and T cell counts over time for slow progressor B57-SP22 are shown in Supplemental Figure 1. HLA alleles published as A*01, A*6802, B*07, B*57, and Cw*07 - note that B*07 differs from earlier reports [Koning2022, PMID: 34880106].

One llama (Capralogics) was immunized subcutaneously with 1 mg of recombinant BG505 DS-SOSIP protein in the presence of Complete Freund’s Adjuvant (CFA) at day 0, and boost immunized with 0.5 mg of BG505 DS-SOSIP protein in the presence of Incomplete Freund’s Adjuvant (IFA) on day 21, 42, 63, 84, 105, 126, 147, 168, 189, 210, 231, and 257. Blood/serum samples were taken on days 0, 52, 73, 94, 115, 136, 157, 178, 199, 220, 241, and 271 for antibody-antigen binding tests and neutralization tests. Whole blood was collected on days 188 and 271, and PBMCs were isolated and used for nanobody phage library construction [Xu2024 PMID: 38704686].

Study subject LSC63 was exposed to HIV-1 via several partners before seroconversion which was 582d after enrollment in the exposed seronegative (ES) cohort in Seattle.  His most recent repeated unprotected sexual relationship lasted 6mo and was with partner, P63 (Patient Rec# 6292), of the UW Primary Infection clinic cohort, who was seropositive for HIV-1  and in turn infected by his previous partner PP63 (Patient Record# 6293, also of the UW Primary Infection clinic cohort). 6 to 12mo prior to seroconversion LSC63 engaged in unprotected sex with 4 other HIV-1 infected partners and 2 partners of unknown serostatus. He developed broad CTL responses to HIV-1 prior to seroconversion, but was likely not infected by his latest partner, P63 (low VL in P63, and genetically unrelated virus in P63 and LSC63). LSC63 did not receive ART up to 2.5 years post-seroconversion and during this time his VL was ˜1000 copies/ml and his CD4+ T-cell count was >500 cells/µl.  He carried the CCR5-Δ32 mutation and the protective B27 HLA allele. [Liu2009b, PMID: 19706711].

Patient 2, of the IrsiCaixa Foundation, was HIV-1-infected, asymptomatic, and ART-naive for 20 years. CD4+ numbers were >500 cells/ml. He had evidence of superinfection, the first case of an LTNP-EC being capable of superinfection. Viral dating showed different dates (1987 for clade A and 1996 for clade B) for the 2 viruses in patient 2. These results strongly suggest that patient 2 was infected with 1 virus and then, 9 years later, became superinfected with another virus, which showed an 11.2% genetic distance from the first virus [Casado2007, PMID: 17703421]. Called LTNP-EC in Pernas2012, he had sustained cellular and humoral responses and carried genetic markers like the CCR5 haplotypes of HHC/HHF*2, and the protective Class II MHC alleles of DRB1*13 and DQB1*16 [Pernas2012, PMID: 16763489]. Patient 2 is known as MDM in later studies, and he had a viral load (VL) of 507 copies/mL and was counted as an elite controller after being infected in 1987 i.e. 35 years prior. He was diagnosed in 1998 though sampling began in 1996 [Perez-Yanes2022, PMID: 35401460].

MG505 was a HIV+ donor of virus to her infant, BG505, in a mother-infant pair. She belonged to a breastfeeding trial and her VL = 29,210 copies/ml. She carried more variants than her infant that were neutralizable by her sera. Her infant, BG505, was born seronegative but tested positive for HIV at week 6 postparturition [Wu2006a, PMID: 16378985]. SOSIP contructs from this patient, MG505.W0.E1 D295N I297T T332N was not neutralizable by bnAb BF520.1 [Simonich2016, PMID: 27345369].

Patient ML851 was resistant/uninfected with HIV-1, though exposed, and carried HLA B*8101, which is part of the B7 superfamily. She recognized DTVLEDINL in 2/5 assays with a peak response of 45 SFU/10ˆ6 PBMCs, recognized TPGPGIRYPL in 3/5 assays with a peak response of 40 SFU/10ˆ6 PBMCs, and recognized FPVTPQVPLR in 1/5 assays with a peak response of 112 SFU/10ˆ6 PBMCs, [Kaul2001, PMID: 11160158]. ML851 was a female Pumwani sex-worker from Nairobi, Kenya who remained persistently seronegative [Rowland-Jones1998, PMID: 33361435]. She carried the HLA B7 gene (this was later specified as B*18:01 in Kaul2001) and recognized epitopes from Nef and p24 [RowlandJones1998b, PMID: 9554277]. ML851 was defined as "resistant"/perisitently seronegative after > 3 years of follow-up and testing by ELISA and PCR for proviral DNA. Here HLA were given as A*68:02, A*01, B*08, B7 (now B*8101) [Rowland-Jones1998, PMID: 9802890].

This patient, Mother 11 in a different paper, was studied for MTCT of her viral isolate, M2, vertically to her daughter who carries viral isolate D2. The mother was infected in 1984 but asymptomatic until  1995 with occasional asthma and began ART in 1995. Details of CD4 and viremic levels over the years are given in Table 1 of Reinis2007 [Reinis2007, PMID: 17331038]. Full-genome RNA sequences were 1993-MI, code WCM32P0793:DQ487190 and 1996-M2, code WCM32P0896: DQ4871. This patient succumbed to an asthma attack in 1998. Mother 11 was homozygous for the wild-type CCR5 gene like her daughter, Daughter1, and shared HLA alleles A19, B44 and Cw16. She birthed a younger daughter,  who was not infected in utero.

MP0471 had a CD4 count of 443 cells/μl and had viral loads of 2,500 and 2,226 copies/ml, respectively, in blood and seminal plasma [Musey2003, PMID: 12477834].

Patient MS, a 25-year old progressor at the time of publishing, was 4-5 months post-seroconversion and had viral loads of <50 and 123 at first presentation-analysis and last available time point respectively; and CD4+ T cell counts of 410 and 696 x 10<sup>6</sup> cells/L at the same time points.  His CTL recognized 3  B*57-restricted epitopes, ISPRTLNAW, TSTLQEQIGW, and YFPDWQNYT, so that 75% of the total tested response was via B*57-restriction (Altfeld2003a).

The male donor (N094) is believed to have acquired HIV infection of subtype CRF01_AE in Thailand and infected the recipient (N107) in the UK. Both partners were diagnosed more than 2 years later when the recipient was HIV-tested during pregnancy [Brener2015, PMID: 26123575].

NVS5, NVS10, and NVS12 are part of the Natural Viral Suppressor (NVS) cohort, a group of HIV-1–infected individuals who suppress HIV-1 replication to <400 copies/mL in the absence of antiretroviral therapy. N12 has had average CD4 counts of 1124 cells/µL over 7 y of infection [Guan2013 PMID: 23237851].
Clade C-infected, chronic patient N106 was ART-naive and showed immune response to several optimal epitopes [Kloverpris2014a, PMID: 24906112].

NVS5, NVS10, and NVS12 are part of the Natural Viral Suppressor (NVS) cohort, a group of HIV-1–infected individuals who suppress HIV-1 replication to <400 copies/mL in the absence of antiretroviral therapy. N12 has had average CD4 counts of 1124 cells/µL over 7 y of infection [Guan2013 PMID: 23237851].

Diagnosis in 2000; when sampled in June 2009 for mAb VRC34.01 the donor had CD4 T-cell count of 463 cells/ml and plasma viral load 4920 RNA copies/ml and was not on antiretroviral treatment; presumed infected with clade B [Kong2016a PMID: 27174988].

N170 is presumed infected with clade B. The mAbs described in Longo2016 (VRC21.01 - VRC29.04) were derived from a serum sample taken in 2010, at which time N170 was 45 yrs old, infected for at least 11 years without ARV treatment, and had a viral load of 7892 copies/ml and a CD4 count of 664 cells/ml.

Volunteer NVS26 was diagnosed with acute retroviral syndrome with 9976 RNA copies/mL and was placed immediately on ARV for 5 y before halting therapy. NVS26 has maintained an average CD4 count of 752 cells/μL and <75 HIV-1 RNA copies/mL for more than 4 y after stopping therapy (the time point of the sample for N26-i1) [Guan2013 PMID: 23237851].
Infected at least one year before antibody derivation; patient was ARV-experienced but had been off ARV for 1 year prior to blood draw used in this study.

Diagnosis in 1984 and sampled 15Oct2007 at age 60; VL was 7,531 copies/ml and CD4+ count was 663 cells/ul; chronically infected and ART naive at the time of study [Zhu2013a PMID 24106303]. While this donor is described in Zhou2013a, no associated antibodies are described.

Patient N49, elite neutralizer, was selected from NVS study. NVS patients are defined as having HIV-1 by western blot while having an HIV-1 RNA < 400 copies/mL for at least 4 measurements and 2 years. N49, a 40 years old (21 years after HIV diagnosis) African American transgender female, had a higher viral load set point, averaging 7,854 HIV-1 copies/mL over 9 years.[Sajadi2018, PMID:29731169].

NVS5, NVS10, and NVS12 are part of the Natural Viral Suppressor (NVS) cohort, a group of HIV-1–infected individuals who suppress HIV-1 replication to <400 copies/mL in the absence of antiretroviral therapy. N12 has had average CD4 counts of 1124 cells/µL over 7 y of infection [Guan2013 PMID: 23237851].

Patient N60, elite neutralizer, was selected from NVS study. NVS patients are defined as having HIV-1 by western blot while having an HIV-1 RNA < 400 copies/mL for at least 4 measurements and 2 years. N60, a 53 year old (5 years after HIV diagnosis) African American male, met the above definition. [Sajadi2018, PMID:29731169].
Asymptomatic adult at time of antibody derivation; no other patient data given [Robinson1990c, PMID: 1694449].

Following the VRC018 clinical trial of a BG505-derived immunogen, virus-uninfected donor N751 showed the highest BG505-reactive ELISA responses. B cells from this donor were sorted for binding to BG505 trimer, and 2 mAbs were selected for characterization (N751-2C06.01 and N751-2C09.01) [Wang2023 PMID:37436899].

At the time point studied for mAb lineage VRC38, this donor was an ARV-naive slow progressor who had been diagnosed 23 years prior and exhibited broad serum neutralization, with viral load of 8,216 copies/ml, and 912 CD4+ T cells/ml [Cale2017, PMID: 28514685]. Patient HIV sequences are subtype B, based on viral sequences deposited in GenBank [Wu2012, PMID: 22419808]. N90 was a North American female who was diagnosed with HIV-1 at 31–32 years of age and who signed informed consent and participated in NIAID protocols; mAb lineage VRC44 was isolated from the same samples (23 years post-diagnosis) used for isolation of lineage VRC38, at which time the participant was therapy-naive [Cale2024, PMID: 39675002]. Cross-reacting mAbs 2526 and 546 which recognize HIV-1 Env and/or influenza HA, SARS-CoV2 spike were isolated from chronically-infected N90 [Vukovich2024, PMID: 39292703].

50-year old chronic subtype B-infected patient NAB01 had comparatively early rebound and moderately decreased viremia. ART (ddl, 3TC, NFV) was begun >24 months post-infection, and VL was at 1,069,200 copies/ml before last ART treatment. Autologous virus used R5 coreceptor. [Trkola2005, PMID: 15880120]. Autologous virus from NAB01 was studied against several Abs inlcuding 447-52D, b12, b6, 17b, 2F5, 4E10, Z13e1, VRC01, 1-79, PGT121, PGT128, [Beauparlant2017, PMID: 28264054]. NAB01's autologous Env lacked the glycosylation site at position 339. For antibodies that recognize patient NAB01's viral Env, 2F5 recognized Env with no mutations; 4E10 recognized nonsynonymous substitutions in Env so that  S671N and D674G had no effect on 4E10 activity; 2G12 recognized T394I in Env which results in a glycosylation site at position 392 [Manrique2007, PMID: 17567707].

41-year old chronic subtype B-infected patient NAB02 underwent ART (ddl, d4T, LPV) begun >24 months post-infection, and VL was at 56,708 copies/ml before last ART treatment. Autologous virus used R5 coreceptor. [Trkola2005, PMID: 15880120].

Infected with Clade B, yet displayed neutralization activity against all genetic subtypes of HIV-1; diagnosis in 1990; sampled in 2006 (for NIH45-46 and VRC01), [Zhou2013a, PMID: 23911655]. Donor 45 is the source of antibody VRC03 and was studied extensively for VH chain evolution, [Wu2011, PMID: 21835983]. ART-naive donor 45 was a slow progressor with CD4 counts >500 cells/μl and pVL <15,000 copies/ml. PBMCs collected in 2008 were the source of many bnAbs [Rudicell2014, PMID: 25142607]. VRC01 class [VH1-2-derived]-antibodies, in particular VRC01, VRC02, VRC03, VRC06, VRC07, VRC08, NIH45-46, were previously isolated from this donor [Zhou2015, PMID: 26004070].

Project: Maple Leaf Medical Clinic, Toronto, Canada
Note: A 43-year old subtype B-positive male participant, OM5001, who had unsuppressed infection for an estimated >14 months, had now been on ART for 9 years, with a VL of 42 copies/ml, and CD4 count of 540 cells/ml. Quantitative viral outgrowth resulted in 4 wells and an IUPM (infectious units per million CD4+ T cells) of 10.46. OM5001's IgG neutralized 9/12 (75%) of a cross-subtype virus panel [Wilson2021; PMID: 34603284].  Time to initiation of ART was 14 months post-infection detection. Viruses isolated from OM5001 were lightly neutralized by MPER bnAbs 2F5, 4E10; moderately neutralized by V1/V2 glycan bnAb PGDM1400 and CD4bs bnAb VRC01. They were well neutralized by V3 Glycan bnAbs PGT121 and 10-1074 and V1/V2 bnAb PG9 as well as CD4bs bnAbs VRC07-523, 3BNC117 and N6. No neutralization of their viruses by V1/V2 bnAb CAP256.VRC26.25 [Ren2018; PMID: 30209173].

Project: Maple Leaf Medical Clinic, Toronto, Canada
Note: A 47-year old subtype B-positive male participant, OM5148, who had unsuppressed infection for an estimated 69 months, had now been on ART for 10 years, with an undetectable VL and CD4 count of 733 cells/ml. Quantitative viral outgrowth resulted in 5 wells and an IUPM (infectious units per million CD4+ T cells) of 1.02. OM5148's IgG neutralized 7/12 (58.3%) of a cross-subtype virus panel [Wilson2021; PMID: 34603284]. Time to initiation of ART was 57 months post-infection detection. Viruses isolated from OM5148 were lightly neutralized by CD4bs bnAbs VRC01, VRC07-523, 3BNC117, N6; MPER bnAbs 10E8, 10E8V4-V5R-100cF, and 4E10; and V1/V2 glycan bnAb PGDM1400. They were well neutralized by V3 Glycan bnAbs PGT121 and 10-1074. No neutralization of their viruses by bnAbs  2G12, CAP256.VRC26.25, PG9, 10E8, 2F5 and 4E10 [Ren2018; PMID: 30209173].

They were not well neutralized by any tested bnAbs. No neutralization of their viruses by CD4bs bnAb VRC01 and MPER bnAbs 2F5 and 4E10 and V3 glycan 2G12 [Ren2018; PMID: 30209173].

HIV-1 subtype B-infected patient P1 of the Options cohort of the University of California, San Francisco, was a male high-risk progressor (CD4 T cell count <750 cells/mm<sup>3</sup>) carrying HLA-B*5701 [Buggert2014, PMID: 24740510]. P27 was recruited through the Xuchang Center for Disease Control and Prevention (Henan Province, China). Donor P27 was selected for B-cell isolation and antibody development due to the serum's exceptionally broad and potent neutralizing activity, which stood out among participants in the study. This donor was classified as a slow progressor, having lived with HIV-1 for 23 years without requiring antiretroviral therapy at the time of leukapheresis. Donor P27’s unique clinical profile made him an ideal candidate for investigating broadly neutralizing antibodies. P27 is also classified by the authors as an elite neutralizer and carries a clade B virus. At the time of lymphocyte apheresis for mAb FD22, P27 had a viral load of 6660 copies/mL and a CD4+ T-cell count of 559 cells/μL. [Wang2025 PMID: 40490455]

Study subject P63, part of the UW Primary Infection Clinic cohort, was found seropositive 2.7 years before the seroconversion of partner LSC63. He was most likely infected by his own previous partner PP63, also of the UW Primary infection Clinic cohort. He likely did not infect LSC63, due to having a low viral load during the relationship and the virus found in LSC63 and P63 being genetically unrelated [Liu2009b, PMID: 19706711].

Project: AIEDRP (Acute Infection and Early Disease Research Program)
Note: Patient 169, a 57-year old male, had a viral load of >750,000 copies/mL and a CD4+T cell count of 412 cells/uL at diagnosis in 1999. Prior to a 9 year post-ART control, he was enrolled into the Acute Infection and Early Disease Research Program study and was started on a regimen of zidovudine, lamivudine, and indinavir within 48 hours of admission. This regimen was changed to abacavir, lamivudine, and efavirenz at week 4. By week 16, his viral load (VL) was <50 copies/mL. He stopped HAART at week 36 for a 2 week period, wherein his viral load rebounded to 22,000 copies/mL. HAART was reinitiated and adhered to until week 92, when it was discontinued entirely. His CD4+ T cell count had been stable over the past 5 years at >1000 cells/uL, and VL was consistently <50 copies/mL since the discontinuation of HAART. In 2011, his VL was measured at 1 copy/mL. He is a confirmed transmission pair with his spouse, who had been diagnosed 3 years before him (Patient record#: 6294, Patient Code: Patient 169 spouse), and had a CD4+ T cell count of 84 cells/uL and baseline viral load prior to HAART of 122,000 copies/mL., This patient is infected with a replication-competent dual tropic CCR5/CXCR4 subtype B virus and despite controlling VL post-treatment he had low titers of bnAbs as well as low CTL HIV-1 suppressive activity while carrying no protective HLA. [Salgado2011, PMID: 22141397].

Patient 169 spouse is a confirmed transmission pair with Patient 169 (Patient code: Patient 169, Patient ID: 2112). This patient was infected with a subtype B virus. [Salgado2011, PMID: 22141397]

Patient 744 is a male Caucasian homosexual who acquired HIV-1 in 1993. His initial CD4 count was 580 cells/μl on day 0 of the study and had declined to 280 by day 323. His HLA type is A24/74; B35/ 44; DR1/12, DR52 [Harcourt 1998].

Born in 1962, diagnosed with HIV in 1989, and had received no ART at the time of antibody derivation; infected with clade B; selected from a group of elite controllers that were followed at the Ragon Institute (PARC) in Boston. Source of clones RU10 and RU11 [Scheid2011]. Patient code given as RU8 and 8ANC131 class [VH1-46-derived]-antibodies, in particular 8ANC131 and 8ANC134, 8ANC37 were previously isolated from this donor [Zhou2015, PMID: 26004070].

ART-naive patient 8 was diagnosed in 1985 and at the time of publication was a 15 year-nonprogressor with CD4+ T cell count = 664 cells/µl, CD8+ T cells = 1,120 cells/µl, plasma HIV RNA = <50-325 copies/ml. The patient elicited CTL response to peptides containing Gag epitopes ISPRTLNAW, KAFSPEVIPMF, TSTLQEQIGW, and QASQEVKNW (Migueles2000).

In 1992, Patient A transmitted HIV-1 to her male sexual partner (Patient B, record #5721) 10 mo after her primary infection. In 1996, she started ART and subsequently maintained undetectable viral loads (<20 copies/ml). In 1998, she was recruited in 2 structured therapy interruption studies and subsequently maintained plasma viremia of <5,000 copies/ml for at least 2 yrs without ART. Dynamics of pVL and CD4 counts are shown in Fig 1. Patient A was heterozygous for CCR5 Δ32 and carried a G<sup>*</sup>A mutation at position 801 in the 3' UTR of the SDF-1 gene. Sequences from paper are deposited in LANL seq. database but were not associated with a specific patient [Arnedo-Valero2004, PMID: 15221891]. LANL database note - this patient is not linked with any CTL epitope record i.e. they have not shown immunogenic response to any database-annotated tested epitope.

In 1992, HIV-1 was transmitted to Patient B by female sexual partner (Patient A, rec# 5720). In 1996, he started ART and subsequently maintained undetectable viral loads (<20 copies/ml). In 1998, he was recruited in 2 structured therapy interruption studies and subsequently maintained plasma viremia of <5,000 copies/ml for at least 2 yrs without ART. Dynamics of pVL and CD4 counts are shown in Fig 1. Patient B was heterozygous for CCR5 Δ32 and HLA alleles were reported as A3101, A68, B4403, and B51. Sequences from paper are deposited in LANL seq. database but were not associated with a specific patient [Arnedo-Valero2004, PMID: 15221891].

Study Subject Bailey2007 was a 35-year old bisexual man who had a relationship with an HIV-1 positive male partner on ART. Infection was estimated to be in October 2002. The patient remained asymptomatic and ART-naive. 3 months later, his CD4+ T cell count was 918 cells/uL and his viral load (VL) was <400 copies/mL. VLs from months 5 and 7 were < 50 copies/mL, but VLs from months 12 and 12 were 69 and 477 copies/mL. At 24 months, VL was 13,870 copies/mL and CD4+ T cell count was 875 cells/uL. He was infected with a replication-competent multiple-drug resistant X4-tropic clade B virus and was HLA-B*57 positive. Loss of virologic control was temporally associated with mutations in B*57-restricted epitopes in Gag. [Bailey2007, PMID: 17538883]

HIV-2-infected 35 yo married man with multiple sexual partners, from Mumbai, India.  He was examined from August 1995 to January 1999 but has been untraceable since then. One HLA-B allele was given as either B*0705 or B*0706.  LANL database note - since no code was given for him, the database has codified this patient as Patient_Kageyama2008. [Kageyama2008, PMID: 18622680].

Spivak2010 described a 59-year old man who presented with AIDS despite repeated negative HIV-1 antibody screening tests. His initial CD4+ T cell count was 90 cells/uL and HIV-1 RNA was > 100,000 copies/mL. The patient was infected with a clade B pansensitive virus. He reported engaging in unprotected sex with a commercial sex worker 2 months before hospital admission. He was started on cART (tenofovir/emtricitabine/efavirenz), and his CD4+ T cell count rose to > 500 cells/uL and his VL fell to 57 copies/mL. 4 months after cART initiation, HIV serology was repeated and was positive. He carried the HLA-B*58:02 allele. [Spivak2010, PMID: 20039801]

Female Donor PC063 was infected with subtype C HIV at 34 years of age after 2 months (7 weeks) of heterosexual contact. PC063 was in the top 20% of neutralizers, with 85% viruses of a 37-virus panel being neturalized [Landais2016, PMID: 26766578] [Personal communication, Dr. Elise Landais]. Her plasma neutralizing-activity peaked at 6 years when 79% of a large panel of viruses were broadly neutralized. This activity was mapped to the CD4 binding site by RSC3 competition. The broadly neutralizing activity in her plasma was also D168R sensitive. [Umotoy2019, PMID:31315032].

IAVI Protocol C was described by Landais et al. [Landais2016, PMID: 26766578] [Personal communication, Dr. Elise Landais], and PC039 is shown among the elite neutralizers. PC39 B-cell response was studied in detail by Joyce et al. [Joyce2023, PMID: 37384622]; PC39 was infected with two similar related founder viruses and went on to mount a broadly neutralizing immune response after 9-11 months of infection.

Source of PCT64 lineage. The donor was enrolled in the IAVI Protocol C longitudinal primary infection cohort approximately 2 weeks after infection by a subtype A1 HIV-1 virus, and was identified as an elite neutralizer [Landais2017, PMID: 29166592] [Personal communication, Dr. Elise Landais].

Sub-Saharan donor, infected with subtype C. Antibodies 33A, 38A and 38B were derived at 33 or 38 months post-infection, as indicated in the antibody name [MacLeod2016, PMID: 27192579]. Serum neutralization was studied by Landais et al. [Landais2016, PMID: 26766578] [Personal communication, Dr. Elise Landais]. PC076 was seropositive for Schistosoma mansoni [Huettner2022, PMID: 35354052].

Subject PG was treated with 2 NA (nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor), 1 PI (protease inhibitor). They had  500 and <50 copies/ml VL and 500 and 565 CD4 cell count, at the time of screening and confirmation respectively. HLA typing was performed at Massachusetts General Hospital. [Altfeld2001, PMID: 11152503].

Controller PY-D5 was ART-naive and 44 years old at the time of study and she carried the protective B*35 HLA allele (either B*35:01 or one of the rarer B*35:40 or B*35:42 or B*35:27 - this was unresolved by typing at the time of publishing).  PY-D5's B*35-NY9-recognizing CTLs were isolated by tetramer staining and six CTL clones generated clonally for study.  Her B*35 HLA were from the PY group which means that its B pocket binds a tyrosine at the PΩ position, and which correlates with a protective, polyfunctional CTL response. [Lobos2024, PMID: 37811811].

Q461 was  part of a cohort of HIV-1-negative women at high risk for HIV-1 infection who were mon- itored at 1 to 2-month intervals for HIV-1 antibody.  She was HIV-1 RNA positive 4 weeks before documented seroconversion, and her PBMCs taken at this pre-seroconversion (considered 28dpi) visit were used. Three PBMC clones Q461c2, Q461d1, and Q461e2 were used to isolate virus. [Long2002, PMID: 12036486]. Viral isolate from Q461d1 was found to carry 2 mutations, T569A and I675V in the gp41 region of Env which conferred an open configuration of Env that caused discontinuous epitope exposure (but not CD4i exposure) making it susceptible to antibody neutralization [Blish2008, PMID: 18177204].

Superinfection. QA013 seroconverted following infection with a clade D virus and was superinfected with a clade A virus between 264 and 385 dpi (Chohan et al., 2005 PMID: 16051862). PBMCs for monoclonal antibodies were selected at 2,282 dpi when her CD4 cell count was 219 cells/mm3 (Williams 2018).

QA255 started out as a subject from a prospective cohort of high-risk HIV-1 seronegative women from Mombasa, Kenya, who acquired subtype A HIV-1 through heterosexual contact, having had one to two sex partners per week; and engaging in sex work to supplement income, but typically not as a primary source of support. She did not report using ART at least until and during this study. [Piantadosi2007, PMID: 18020705]. 4.07 years after infection, QA255, infected with subtype A, had a VL of 4.8, and a calculated neutralizing breadth of 5.3 [Piantadosi2009, PMID: 19640996].  Elite Neturalizer QA255 was one of the broadest and one of the most potent neutralizers from the cohort under study by 4.7 years post infection [Cortez2012, PMID: 22479183]. QA255 was considered to be a FSW (female sexworker) who was  singly infected, and generated HIV-1–specific nAbs by ∼5 years post-infection, becoming a top, broad neutralizer. She was 45.1 years of age at the time of this study, and had been plasma positive for 5.1 years; with serum cross-reactivity against subtypes B, C, and CRF01_03 [Goo2014, PMID: 24859529]. Participant QA255 developed early cross-clade neutralizing activity and was enrolled in a prospective cohort of HIV-1 negative high-risk women in Mombasa, Kenya. QA255 seroconverted after 4.8 years of follow-up. IgG was purified from 6 plasma samples obtained from QA255 between 89 and 914 days post-infection; she remained drug naive during this period. Infected by subtype A1 HIV-1. [Williams2015, PMID: 26629541]

Elite Neturalizer QB850 was the broadest and one of the most potent neutralizers from the cohort under study by 4.7 years post infection [Cortez2012, PMID: 22479183]. QB850 was a FSW (female sexworker) who was superinfected, and generated HIV-1–specific nAbs by ∼5 years postinfection, becoming a top, broad neutralizer [Goo2014, PMID: 24859529]. Superinfection with A1 and D strains [Cortez2015, PMID: 26158467]. [Piantadosi2007, PMID: 18020705].

Progression: Elite Controller (EC); Elite Neutralizer (EN); Long Term Non-Progressor (LTNP)
Note: Patient infected with B clade; identified from a cohort of drug-naive patients, and selected for study based on a high level of serum bnAbs. Male donor SA003 (year of birth 1963), ART naive, was selected among a cohort of HIV-1-infected patients enrolled in the Hospital Sacco in Milan due to the ability of the patient's plasma to neutralize 8 out of 9 HIV-1 PVs of the Global Panel. At the time of sampling for Ab LN01, SA003 was a Long Term Non-Progressor or elite controller patient who consented to a lymphadenectomy. At that time, he had been infected with HIV-1 for 30 years with CD4 T-cell counts of 474 cells/ml and plasma HIV-1 RNA values of 1600 copies/ml, CDC stage A1. The donor was not involved in previous procedures and he had never received antiretroviral therapy. [Pinto2019 PMID 31653483].

Donor SC2 is a 36-year-old homosexual male with a history of regular unprotected sexual intercourse with men known to be HIV-seropositive. A previous HIV antibody test (Jan. 10, 1995) had been negative. In October 1995, he was admitted to hospital with a sore throat, fever, headache, and photophobia; symptoms persisted until mid-November. Blood sampled on Oct. 13, 1995 tested HIV p24 antigen-positive (>100 pg/ml), antibody-negative (determined by standard ELISA methods; Abbott); a second sample 6 days later tested HIV p24 antigen-positive (>500 pg/ml), HIV gp41 antibody-positive, and HIV p31 antibody-, p24 antibody-, p17 antibody- and gp36 antibody-negative. Together these results imply that donor SC2 acquired HIV-1 in September or early October 1995.  On 19Oct1995, CD4+ count was 384 x 10<sup>6</sup>/L and VL(viral load) was 7,600,000 copies/mL; on 03Nov1995, CD4+ count was 518 x 10<sup>6</sup>/L; on 03Jan1996, VL was 715,000; on 02Apr1996, CD4+ count was 330 x 10<sup>6</sup>/L and VL was 700,000. CTL lines were generated from him and his proviral sequence was seen to vary. [Price1997, PMID: 9050875]. Donor SC2's HIV Nef was sequenced between Oct 1995 and June 1996 and variability calculated in escape FLKEnGGL from FLKEKGGL [Goulder1997e, PMID: 9416500]. Patient SC2 of the Chelsea and Westminster Hospital (London) was a Caucasian homosexual male between the ages of 25-37, with recent exposure to HIV-1 though high-risk sexual encounters followed by seroconversion. At 7 DFOSx, CD4 and CD8 T cell counts were 384/2016 cells/mL.; at 22 DFOSx, 518/5994 cells/mL; at 172 DFOSx, 330/840; at d253, 325/0; at d543, 475/1143; at d605, 401/786; at d606, 449/993; at d718, 588/1013; at d760, 532/3835; at d1059, 395/860; at d1083, 451/1246; at d1163, 351/680 and at 1340 DFOSx, 465/1012 cells/mL. [Oxenius2000b, PMID: 10737796]. LANL database note - HLA were published as A1, B7/8, Cw0701, Cw0702, DR4/53, DQ7; we have updated the alleles to reflect the current, 2010 genotypic nomenclature. Longitudinal analyses indicates positive selection in epitope FL8 [daSilva2003, PMID: 15046259]. Rate of escape of a reported CTL escape variant was estimated from longitudinal data. The patient was in primary infection when samples were collected. When the sampling period began, viral load was 7,600,000 copies/mL and CD4+ T cell count was 384 cells/mm<sup>3</sup>. [Asquith2006, PMID: 16515366]
Donor SC7 was HIV-positive and virtually a non responder to both Gag and Pol epitopes (2 Gag epitopes, 0 Pol epitopes) [Goulder1997, PMID: 9126923].

Slow progressor, HIV-1-infected volunteer 012 first tested positive in 1985 with a CD4 count of 218 cells/µl, VL of 77,000 cell/ml. This HLA-B*08+ patient had a response to epitope FLKEKGGL [Papagno2002, PMID: 12452843].

Patient SP-1 is a slow progressor who was diagnosed in 1982, infected as a hemophiliac, with CD4 count at 1006 cells/mL and VL at 2,599 copies/mL.  He recognized two novel epitopes, B*40-restricted Gag LETSEGCRQI and B*58 Nef GYFPDWQNY. B*58-restricted Nef HTQGYFPDW and Gag GVGGPGHKARV (prediction A*02) and TERQANFL (prediction B*40) were known epitopes he recognized.  Predicted epitopes Nef KEKGGLEGL and Gag ILGQLQPSL, Gag ISPRTLNAW with predicted restrictions of B*40, A*02 and B*58 respectively. He also recognized peptide Gag GKKKYKLKHIVWASR [Migueles2015, PMID: 26137533].

SUMA or Patient 4 infected at age 22, presented to University of Alabama at Birmingham hospital. 6 days before transmission to WEAU Patient 1, SUMA's CD4 count was 42 cells/ml [Clark1991, PMID: 1900576] and [Graziosi1993, PMID: 8341646]. SUMA established a low persisting VL (2,258 HIV-1 RNA copies/ml at 278 DFOSx) and maintained a high CD4 count (722 cells/ml throughout 7 yrs of follow-up [Jones2004, PMID: 15545352] At CDC stage 1 of infection, there were 853 CD4+ cells/ml, and 1.5x10<sup>6</sup> HIV-1 RNA copies/ml. [Piatak1993, PMID: 8096089]. Initial HLA serotyping was from Borrow 1994 and infectious virus was first cleared from plasma only 20d after onset of acute retroviral syndrome, with reappearance in plasma not yet determined [Borrow1994, PMID: 8057491]. HLA genotype also as a personal communication from Emma Turnbull [PTH 6/09]. Caucasian male SUMA 0874 was a symptomatic, seronegative, Fiebig Stage II - 23 year old at screening for enrollment, with a viral set point of 12,507 RNA copies per ml and CD4+ median count of 952 cells/µL.  He self-reported as a male having sex with male and no history of intravenous drug use [Goonetilleke2009, PMID: 19487423]. Data from this patient is also synonymous with Dataset 21. Rate of escape for a reported CTL escape variant was estimated from longitudinal data from Jones2004. When the sample was collected in Jones2004, the patient was in primary infection with a viral load of  1,000,000 copies/mL and CD4+ T cell count of 900 cells/mm<sup>3</sup> [Asquith2006, PMID: 16515366].

T125 was born in Sri Lanka, but was sampled in 2009 at the age of 37 yrs as part of a diverse East London cohort. At that time, he had a VL of 73,700 and had been infected for >1 yr but was ART-naive. T125 was an elite neutralizer and the source of bnAb ELC07, but was not an elite controller or LTNP. Memory B cells, harvested from 2 timepoints 4 mo apart, also at a time >1 yr post-infection and while ART-naive, were notable in that they were more similar to memory B cells from uninfected rather than infected people [Griffith2024, PMID: 38370662].

T282 was part of an elite-neutralizing transmission pair; they had broad serum neutralization that was heritable between transmitting (T282) and recipient (R282) partners [Kouyos2018, PMID: 30202088]. Macaque infected with SHIV.CH1012 (subtype C env) developed a bnAb lineage, RHA10, targeting the CD4bs [Morris2025 PMID: 40516049].

Macaque T925 sera in weeks 12-34 after inoculation developed increasing neutralization titers against the immunizing SIV-Cam13K, and potent neutralization of Tier 1 heterologous viruses [Bibollet-Ruche2023 PMID: 36629414].

Patient ID TM16 was a 29 year old C-clade-infected, asymptomatic, ART-naive individual from South Africa with CD4 count of 702 cells/µl, viral load (VL) of 15,000 copies/ml, and who had an immune response to peptides from the Zembe2011 study.  [Zembe2011; PMID: 22022524].

Macaque was both vaccinated (strains BG505 and C consensus) and infected by SHIV.Ce1176 prior to assessing serum and mAb responses; responses were directed at the fusion peptide [Wang2024b PMID: 39471811].

Subject TW1 was a monozygotic twin to TW2 and younger brother by three years to BR3. TW1 and TW2 shared needles with the same HIV+ person at the same time and both had negative HIV-1 ELISA in December 1998 but a seropositive result in September 1999, being diagnosed at age 21. TW1 was HCV co-infected and ART-naive. All 3 brothers were infected by the same viral strain, shared similar viral loads, but CD4+ decline was slower in the twins. TW1 succumbed to a drug overdose at year three post-infection, [Draenert2006, PMID: 16533886].

Subject TW2 was a monozygotic twin to TW1 and younger brother by three years to BR3. TW1 and TW2 shared needles with the same HIV+ person at the same time and both had negative HIV-1 ELISA in December 1998 but a seropositive result in September 1999, being diagnosed at age 21. TW2 was HCV co-infected and ART-naive. All 3 brothers were infected by the same viral strain, shared similar viral loads, but CD4+ decline was slower in the twins, [Draenert2006, PMID: 16533886].

Donor U1 was a persistently seronegative member of a discordant couple in a cohort of nine HIV serodiscordant couples recruited from the Genito-Urinary Medicine clinic at St Mary's Hospital, London (i.e. couples in a stable relationship of at least 4 years duration, of whom one of the pair is HIV-infected and whose partner remains HIV-1 seronegative despite documented exposure). They responded strongly but transiently to a B44-restricted peptide from Gag p24 and an A29-restricted peptide from Env gp120 [RowlandJones2001, PMID: 11595285].

Donor U2 was a persistently seronegative member of a discordant couple in a cohort of nine HIV serodiscordant couples recruited from the Genito-Urinary Medicine clinic at St Mary's Hospital, London (i.e. couples in a stable relationship of at least 4 years duration, of whom one of the pair is HIV-infected and whose partner remains HIV-1 seronegative despite documented exposure). They responded strongly but transiently to an A1-restricted peptide from Gag p17; two B*08-restricted peptides from Env gp41; an A1-restricted peptide from Rev; a B8-restricted peptide from Nef; and B8- and B57-restricted peptides from Pol [RowlandJones2001, PMID: 11595285].

Participant VA02 (NIH-related cohort) was a non-drug using, non-hemophiliac HIV-1-infected adult from a geographically distinct location than the Immunoco PS participants. Donor VA02 intiated ART therapy 11 years after diagnosis, and for 11 years from February 2008 to September 2019. Their Gag-KK10-specific CTLs expressed CD28 with greater frequency over time, along with gene transcription changes that gave them a more stem-like profile. [White2024, PMID: 39179934].

Patient VC1, a viremic controller, had CD4 count 1086 cells/µl and HIV RNA 263 copies/mL. They were negative for KIR3DS1 alleles. This subject was used as a control for comparison in Walker-Sperling2017, PMID: 28159573.

Subjects 10002, 10028, and 10076 were infected chronically with HIV, with a duration of infection from 7 to 24 years, and with CD4+T cell counts >500 at the time of B cell sorting. These subjects were treatment-naive. 10002 was infected in 1984, and was the source of 7 mAbs (not annotated) [Hicar2016 PMID: 26748387]. These 3 patients and their polyclonal antibodies were part of the Vanderbilt cohort, first described by Sather2010; on a clade B reference neutralization panel, serum from Subject 10002 neutralized 47% of isolates [Sather2010 PMID: 20510750].

Followed longitudinally over time since infection. This patient is dual-infected with HIV-1 subtype B and HCV [Sather2009, PMID:18987148]. At time of recruitment into Vanderbilt Cohort (part of TN-CFAR), subject had CD4+ T cell counts of >250/μl without AIDS-defining illness and had no history of ART or antiviral treatment. Samples used for study were collected on 9/26/2003 (53 yrs old) and 3/21/2006 (56 yrs old), approximately 6 months and >3 yrs, respectively, after onset of co-infection [Pilewski2023, PMID: 36708513]. Glycoprotein immunogens from VC10014 viruses were used to elicit mAbs in rabbit [Malherbe2014 PMID:25210191] and in macaque.  VC10014 developed neutralization breadth within <2 years of infection  25257 [Spencer2021 PMID:33472907].

These 3 patients and their polyclonal antibodies were part of the Vanderbilt cohort, first described by Sather2010 [PMID: 20510750]. Elite controller, patient VC10042 developed an unusually broad and potent cross-neutralizing antibody response. Some circulating viruses isolated 19- and 22-years post-infection however, evolved to escape bnAb action, especially CD4bs antibodies. 1/12 such autologous HIV-1 clones isolated was susceptible to Nab VRC01, but 8/12 of them were moderately susceptible to NIH45-46<sup>G54W</sup>. All 12 clones were neutralized with 10x potency using Nab NIH45-46m2. [DATABASE NOTE: this patient's Env clones and polyclonal sera have been studied, but the patient has not been the source for monoclonal Abs or epitopes. For more information, see Sather2009, Sather2012 and Carbonetti2014]

WEAU or Patient 1, infected at age 29, presented to University of Alabama at Birmingham hospital. 16 and 40 days after onset of symptoms, CD4 count was 358 and 971 cells/mL respectively. His Transmission partner was SUMA or Patient 4 [Clark1991, PMID: 1900576]. WEAU established high persisting VL (90,109 HIV-1 RNA copies/mL at 212 DFOSx) and had a steep decline in CD4 cell count after 40 DFOSx to 30 cells/ml within 2 yrs [Jones2004, PMID: 15545352].  At CDC stage 1 of infection, there were 358 CD4+ cells/ml, and 3.6 x10<sup>5</sup> HIV-1 RNA copies/mL.  [Piatak1993, PMID: 8096089]. Initial HLA serotyping from Borrow 1994 and infectious virus was first cleared from plasma 23d after onset of acute retroviral syndrome, with reappearance in plasma at day 368 [Borrow1994, PMID: 8057491]. Data from this patient is also synonymous with Datasets 5/19/20. Rate of escape of two reported CTL escape variants was estimated from longitudinal data from Jones2004. The patient was in primary infection when all samples were collected. When the Env sampling period began, viral load was 34,700 copies/mL and CD4+ T cell count was 900 cells/mm<sup>3</sup>, and when the Tat sampling period began, viral load was 216,400 copies/mL and CD4+ T cell count was 358 cells/mm<sup>3</sup>. HLA reported by Jones2004 as A*2902, B*0801, B*4403. Rate of escape was also estimated of a reported CTL escape variant from longitudinal data from Borrow1997. When the sample was collected, viral load was 146,800 copies/mL and CD4+ T cell count was 748 cells/mL. [Asquith2006, PMID: 16515366]. Initial HLA serotyping was from Borrow1994 [Borrow1994, PMID: 8057491]. Certain variants of virus "WEAU" are found in this patient too [Borrow1997, PMID: 9018240] and [Borrow1998, PMID: 9795762].

Note: Z1800M was identified shortly after virus transmission; among 21 patients studied, Z100M had the highest plasma neutralizing capacity against autologous virus and high sequence diversity at 3 years post-infection; HLA alleles are both A*30:02 [Smith2016a, PMID:27851829]. Among 42 patients assessed, PC068 serum had the greatest breadth, with 97% of a 37-virus panel being neutralized [Landais2016, PMID: 26766578] [Personal communication, Dr. Elise Landais].

Participant Z258 took part in a NIAID study at the NIH; and was infected with subtype B [Doria-Rose2009, PMID 18922865]. The patient's serum had broad and potent neutralizing ability. He was chronically infected (21 years) and drug naive at the time of sampling for mAbs VRC27 and N6 [Huang2016a, PMID: 27851912]. VRC01 class [VH1-2-derived]-antibodies, in particular VRC27 was previously isolated from this donor [Zhou2015, PMID: 26004070].
